message.content: Adversaries may exploit print processors to achieve persistence and/or privilege escalation by running malicious DLLs during system boot. Print processors, which are DLLs loaded by the print spooler service, can be manipulated to load malicious code at startup. This can be done by adding print processors via the AddPrintProcessor API call with SeLoadDriverPrivilege or by modifying specific registry keys to point to the malicious DLL. The print processor must be located in the system print-processor directory, and the print spooler service must be restarted for the changes to take effect. Since the print spooler service operates with SYSTEM level permissions, any malicious print processor will run with elevated privileges. Notable adversaries like Earth Lusca, Gelsemium, and PipeMon have exploited this technique by modifying registry keys to load malware as print processors. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting the use of SeLoadDriverPrivilege, auditing and securing registry keys related to print processors, and ensuring that only legitimate print processors are present in the system directory. Regularly monitoring the print spooler service for unusual activity can also help detect and prevent exploitation.
